title: Activation in Vesicle-Mediated Signaling Shaped by Batch Arrival Statistics
authors: Jan Hauke, Julian B. Voits, Ulrich S. Schwarz
abstract: Vesicle-mediated secretion of ions or molecules is a central mechanism of cellular communication, for example in processes such as neurotransmission or hormone release. These events are inherently stochastic: vesicle fusions lead to bursts of variable sizes, releasing discrete packets of transmitters that are subsequently cleared or degraded. The dynamics break time-reversal symmetry due to the interplay of spontaneous bursts and continuous degradation. Using generating functions and a recursion relation, we derive an exact solution for the full time-dependent probability distribution of a general batch arrival-degradation model. This framework also enables a full analysis of first-passage times to a concentration threshold representing downstream activation. We show that activation kinetics are not determined by mean dynamics alone, but depend sensitively on the temporal statistics of arrival events, batch-size variability, and degradation. In particular, different arrival processes with identical mean rates can lead to qualitatively distinct first-passage behavior, reflecting the role of time-asymmetric fluctuations. We also discuss extensions incorporating vesicle depletion. Our results provide a transparent link between stochastic release dynamics and activation timing in vesicle-mediated signaling.
summary: This paper provides an exact mathematical framework to describe how the timing of cellular activation is shaped by the randomness of molecular release. It demonstrates that the variability in the timing and size of release events can lead to qualitatively different activation behaviors even when mean rates are identical. The work also accounts for the effects of vesicle depletion and replenishment on signaling dynamics.
main_problem: Understanding how the stochastic nature of vesicle-mediated signaling, specifically the temporal statistics of arrival events and burst-size variability, shapes the timing of downstream cellular activation.
main_result: The authors derive an exact solution for the time-dependent probability distribution of a batch arrival-degradation model, showing that activation kinetics depend on time-asymmetric fluctuations rather than just mean dynamics.
method: The study employs a master equation approach solved via generating functions, recursion relations, and Bell polynomials, alongside a stochastic sampling algorithm for trajectories.
model_or_system: A general batch arrival-degradation process modeling vesicle-mediated secretion (e.g., neurotransmission) where molecules are released in discrete bursts and subsequently degraded at a rate proportional to the current count.
key_observables: Time-dependent occupation probabilities, first-passage times (FPT) to a concentration threshold, mean and variance of molecule counts, and recurrence times.
important_parameters: Arrival rate (kappa), degradation rate (gamma), burst-size distribution (q_m), inter-arrival time statistics (Poisson vs. fixed-interval), and vesicle replenishment rate (xi).
main_assumptions: The primary model assumes a Poisson process for arrivals and linear degradation; the extended model assumes a finite number of docking sites with a specific replenishment rate.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the biological context and the stochastic nature of molecule counts; Figure 2 shows simulated probability distributions; Figure 4 compares fixed and Poisson arrival trains; Figure 7 shows the impact of replenishment on steady-state distributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the biological problem, derives the exact solution for the master equation using generating functions, extends the model to include vesicle depletion, compares different arrival statistics (Poisson vs. fixed-interval), and concludes with a discussion on biological implications.
why_interesting: The mathematical framework of non-equilibrium, time-asymmetric stochastic processes and the derivation of exact solutions for threshold-crossing in bursty systems are highly relevant to open quantum systems and quantum optics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Stochastic burst–degradation dynamics in cellular communication. A Synaptic signaling: Neurotransmitter molecules are released in discrete batches from synaptic vesicles into the synaptic cleft following neuronal stimulation. Molecules diffuse, bind to postsynaptic receptors, and are cleared by uptake or enzymatic degradation. B Endocrine signaling: Hormone-containing vesicles in an endocrine cell release bursts of molecules into the bloodstream. The hormones are transported, degraded, or diluted, and activate distant target cells once the local concentration exceeds an effective threshold. C The molecule count in these systems becomes a stochastic variable (blue line) that is...

Figure 2. Simulated distribution of the number of molecules pn(t) for λ(t) = 1 and qn = P∞ v=0 δn,1000vBinom(v|N = 50, p = 0.2), which means that each vesicle can bring up to 50 new molecules with a release probability of 20 percent. One sees that the system first grows in bursts due to the initial condition of no molecules, and then relaxes again.

Figure 3. Release size distribution qm for quantal count distribution q(V ) = Binom(V, ρ=0.8) and quantal size distribution q(C) = N(⟨c⟩, σc = 0.1⟨c⟩). The dashed line represents a normal distribution with mean ⟨m⟩= ⟨c⟩⟨v⟩ and variance σ2 m as in Eq. (23).

Figure 4. Steady state, hitting probabilities and passage times for the fixed-interval train (blue) and the Poisson train (orange). The count of released vesicles is assumed to follow a binomial distribution with V = 50 release sites and release probability ρ = 0.2. The quantal size distribution is normal with mean ⟨c⟩= 1000 molecules per vesicle and σc = 0.2⟨c⟩. The quartiles of the shown distributions were obtained from stochastic sampling (see Supplement III). A Example trajectories for a fixed train (F) and a Poisson train (P). B Post-release distribution π+ n and steady state distribution πn. The steady-state distribution of the fixed train depends on the time T since the last release...

Figure 5. Process where the release rate decays exponentially with rate γ and the total number of release events follows a Poisson distribution with mean N. The release size distribution is the same as in Fig. 4. A Example trajectories. B Distribution of maximum threshold reached, compared to the ”deterministic” maximum of the mean molecule count in gray. C First-passage time to threshold, provided that the threshold is reached, compared to the transient of the mean molecule count in gray.
